fields.explanation: The Act was passed in 2019 to provide reservation in teachers' cadre in central educational institutions.

fields.text: The National Council of Educational Research and Training (NCERT) was established in:
fields.explanation: NCERT was established in 1961 to assist and advise the Government of India on educational policies and programmes.

fields.text: The University Grants Commission (UGC) was established in:
fields.explanation: UGC was established in 1956 to coordinate, determine and maintain standards of university education.

fields.text: The All India Council for Technical Education (AICTE) was established in:
fields.explanation: AICTE was established in 1945 but was given statutory status in 1987 to regulate technical education.

fields.text: The Distance Education Council (DEC) was established in:
fields.explanation: DEC was established in 1985 to promote and coordinate distance education in India.

fields.text: The National Institute of Educational Planning and Administration (NIEPA) was established in:
fields.explanation: NIEPA was established in 1970 as a specialized institution for educational planning and administration.

fields.text: The Central Board of Secondary Education (CBSE) was established in:
fields.explanation: CBSE was established in 1962, though its predecessor existed since 1929.

fields.text: The National Institute of Open Schooling (NIOS) was established in:
fields.explanation: NIOS was established in 1989 to provide flexible education through open and distance learning.

fields.text: The Indian Council of Historical Research (ICHR) was established in:
fields.explanation: ICHR was established in 1972 to promote historical research and to bring historians together.

fields.text: The Indian Council of Social Science Research (ICSSR) was established in:
fields.explanation: ICSSR was established in 1969 to promote social science research in India.

fields.text: The Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR) was established in:
fields.explanation: ICAR was established in 1929 to promote agricultural research and education in India.

fields.text: The Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR) was established in:
fields.explanation: CSIR was established in 1942 to promote scientific and industrial research in India.

fields.text: The Department of Atomic Energy (DAE) was established in:
fields.explanation: DAE was established in 1954 under the direct charge of the Prime Minister to develop nuclear technology.

fields.text: The Department of Space (DOS) was established in:
fields.explanation: DOS was established in 1972 to promote the development and application of space science and technology.

fields.text: The National Informatics Centre (NIC) was established in:
fields.explanation: NIC was established in 1976 to provide e-governance support and IT solutions to the government.

fields.text: The Centre for Development of Advanced Computing (C-DAC) was established in:
fields.explanation: C-DAC was established in 1988 to develop advanced computing technologies and solutions.

fields.text: The National Institute of Immunology (NII) was established in:
fields.explanation: NII was established in 1984 to conduct research in immunology and related areas.

fields.text: The National Brain Research Centre (NBRC) was established in:
fields.explanation: NBRC was established in 1998 to conduct research in neuroscience and brain-related disorders.

fields.text: The National Centre for Cell Science (NCCS) was established in:
fields.explanation: NCCS was established in 1988 to promote cell and molecular biology research.

fields.text: The National Institute of Virology (NIV) was established in:
fields.explanation: NIV was established in 1952 to conduct research on viral diseases and develop vaccines.

fields.text: The National Institute of Epidemiology (NIE) was established in:
fields.explanation: NIE was established in 2000 to strengthen epidemiological research and training in India.

fields.text: The National Institute of Cholera and Enteric Diseases (NICED) was established in:
fields.explanation: NICED was established in 1962 to conduct research on cholera and enteric diseases.

fields.text: The National Institute of Mental Health and Neurosciences (NIMHANS) was established in:
fields.explanation: NIMHANS was established in 1974 by merging two institutions to provide comprehensive mental health care.

fields.text: The Jawaharlal Nehru Centre for Advanced Scientific Research (JNCASR) was established in:
fields.explanation: JNCASR was established in 1989 to conduct advanced research in science and technology.

fields.text: The Indian Statistical Institute (ISI) was established in:
fields.explanation: ISI was established in 1931 by Professor P.C. Mahalanobis to promote statistical research.

fields.text: The Tata Institute of Fundamental Research (TIFR) was established in:
fields.explanation: TIFR was established in 1945 by Dr. Homi Bhabha to conduct research in fundamental sciences.

fields.text: The Indian Institute of Science (IISc) was established in:
fields.explanation: IISc was established in 1909 with the support of Jamsetji Tata and the Government of India.

fields.text: The first Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) was established in:
fields.explanation: The first IIT was established in Kharagpur in 1951.

fields.text: The first Indian Institute of Management (IIM) was established in:
fields.explanation: The first IIM was established in Ahmedabad in 1961.

fields.text: The All India Institute of Medical Sciences (AIIMS) was established in:
fields.explanation: AIIMS New Delhi was established in 1956 as an autonomous institution under the Ministry of Health.

fields.text: The National Law School of India University (NLSIU) was established in:
fields.explanation: NLSIU was established in 1988 in Bangalore as the first national law school in India.

fields.text: Which of the following is the brain of a computer?
fields.explanation: The CPU (Central Processing Unit) is called the brain of the computer as it executes instructions and performs calculations.

fields.text: What does RAM stand for?
fields.explanation: RAM stands for Random Access Memory, which is the computer's primary memory used to store data temporarily.

fields.text: Which of the following is NOT an input device?
fields.explanation: Printer is an output device, not an input device. It produces hard copies of digital documents.

fields.text: Which generation of computers used integrated circuits?
fields.explanation: Third generation computers (1965-1971) used integrated circuits (ICs) instead of transistors.

fields.text: The file extension .exe is associated with:
fields.explanation: .exe extension is used for executable files in Windows operating system.

fields.text: Which key combination is used to copy text in Windows?
fields.explanation: Ctrl + C is the keyboard shortcut for copying selected text or objects in Windows.

fields.text: The process of starting a computer is called:
fields.explanation: Booting is the process of starting a computer and loading the operating system into memory.

fields.text: Which of the following is a Linux distribution?
fields.explanation: Ubuntu is a popular Linux distribution based on Debian.

fields.text: What does URL stand for?
fields.explanation: URL stands for Uniform Resource Locator, which is the address of a web page.

fields.text: What does ISP stand for?
fields.explanation: ISP stands for Internet Service Provider, a company that provides internet access to customers.

fields.text: Which of the following is NOT a feature of Microsoft Word?
fields.explanation: Pivot Tables are a feature of Microsoft Excel, not Microsoft Word.

fields.text: In Microsoft Excel, what does the function SUM() do?
fields.explanation: The SUM() function in Excel adds up all the numbers in a range of cells.

fields.text: Which software is primarily used for creating presentations?
fields.explanation: Microsoft PowerPoint is primarily used for creating and delivering presentations.

fields.text: What is the file extension for a Microsoft Word document?
fields.explanation: .docx is the file extension for Microsoft Word documents in newer versions.

fields.text: Which of the following is used to create and manage databases?
fields.explanation: Microsoft Access is a database management system used to create and manage databases.

fields.text: What does HTML stand for?
fields.explanation: HTML stands for Hyper Text Markup Language, used for creating web pages.

fields.text: Which symbol is used to start a comment in Python?
fields.explanation: In Python, the hash symbol (#) is used to start a single-line comment.

fields.text: What does CSS stand for?
fields.explanation: CSS stands for Cascading Style Sheets, used for styling web pages.

fields.text: Which of the following is NOT a database management system?
fields.explanation: Photoshop is image editing software, not a database management system.

fields.text: What is malware?
fields.explanation: Malware is malicious software designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorized access to computer systems.

fields.text: Which of the following is a type of malware?
fields.explanation: Virus, Trojan, and Worm are all types of malware that can harm computer systems.

fields.text: What is phishing?

options.C: Fraudulent attempt to obtain sensitive information

fields.explanation: Phishing is a fraudulent attempt to obtain sensitive information by disguising as a trustworthy entity.

fields.text: What is a firewall?

options.B: A security system that monitors network traffic

fields.explanation: A firewall is a security system that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic.

fields.text: Which of the following is a strong password characteristic?

options.C: Contains a mix of letters, numbers, and symbols

fields.explanation: A strong password should contain a mix of uppercase and lowercase letters, numbers, and special symbols.

fields.text: What is the full form of UPI?
fields.explanation: UPI stands for Unified Payment Interface, a real-time payment system in India.

fields.text: Which of the following is a digital payment platform?
fields.explanation: Paytm, PhonePe, and Google Pay are all digital payment platforms operating in India.

fields.text: What is Aadhaar?
fields.explanation: Aadhaar is a 12-digit unique identity number issued to Indian residents based on biometric data.

fields.text: Which portal is used for filing income tax returns online in India?
fields.explanation: The e-Filing portal is used for filing income tax returns online in India.

fields.text: What is DigiLocker?
fields.explanation: DigiLocker is a digital document storage platform launched by the Government of India.

fields.text: What does GPS stand for?
fields.explanation: GPS stands for Global Positioning System, used for navigation and location tracking.

fields.text: Which of the following is a mobile operating system?
fields.explanation: Android, iOS, and Windows Phone are all mobile operating systems.

fields.text: What is Bluetooth?
fields.explanation: Bluetooth is a wireless communication technology for short-range data exchange.

fields.text: Which generation of mobile technology supports 4G?
fields.explanation: 4G (Fourth Generation) mobile technology provides high-speed internet access.

fields.text: What does Wi-Fi stand for?
fields.explanation: Wi-Fi stands for Wireless Fidelity, a wireless networking technology.

fields.text: What is Artificial Intelligence (AI)?

options.B: Machine intelligence that mimics human cognitive functions

fields.explanation: AI is machine intelligence that can perform tasks typically requiring human intelligence.

fields.text: What is cloud computing?

options.B: Delivery of computing services over the internet

fields.explanation: Cloud computing is the delivery of computing services over the internet.

fields.text: What does IoT stand for?
fields.explanation: IoT stands for Internet of Things, referring to interconnected devices that can communicate.

fields.text: What is blockchain technology primarily known for?
fields.explanation: Blockchain technology is primarily known for its use in cryptocurrency and secure, transparent transactions.

fields.text: What is machine learning?

options.B: A subset of AI that learns from data

fields.explanation: Machine learning is a subset of AI that enables systems to learn and improve from data without explicit programming.

fields.text: What is Big Data?
fields.explanation: Big Data refers to extremely large and complex datasets that require special tools and techniques to process.

fields.text: What is data mining?

options.A: Digging for data in the ground
options.B: Extracting useful information from large datasets

fields.explanation: Data mining is the process of discovering patterns and extracting useful information from large datasets.

fields.text: What is a database?
fields.explanation: A database is a collection of organized data that can be easily accessed, managed, and updated.

fields.text: What does SQL stand for?
fields.explanation: SQL stands for Structured Query Language, used for managing and querying databases.

fields.text: What is data backup?

options.B: Creating copies of data for protection

fields.explanation: Data backup is the process of creating copies of data to protect against data loss.

fields.text: What is bandwidth in networking?

options.A: The width of a network cable
options.C: The number of computers in a network

fields.explanation: Bandwidth refers to the maximum data transfer rate of a network or internet connection.

fields.text: What does LAN stand for?
fields.explanation: LAN stands for Local Area Network, a network that connects devices within a limited area.

fields.text: What is the difference between HTTP and HTTPS?

options.B: HTTPS is secure, HTTP is not

fields.explanation: HTTPS is the secure version of HTTP, providing encryption for data transmission.

fields.text: What is an IP address?

options.B: A unique identifier for devices on a network

fields.explanation: An IP address is an Internet Protocol address that uniquely identifies devices on a network.

fields.text: What does VPN stand for?
fields.explanation: VPN stands for Virtual Private Network, providing secure connections over the internet.

fields.text: What is the full form of JAM Trinity?
fields.explanation: JAM Trinity refers to Jan Dhan (bank accounts), Aadhaar (unique ID), and Mobile (connectivity).

fields.text: Which portal is used for government job applications in India?
fields.explanation: National Career Service (NCS) is the official portal for government job applications in India.

fields.text: What is the purpose of the UMANG app?
fields.explanation: UMANG (Unified Mobile Application for New-age Governance) provides access to government services.

fields.text: What is e-Sign?
fields.explanation: e-Sign is an electronic signature service that allows users to digitally sign documents using Aadhaar.

fields.text: What is the Digital India initiative?

options.B: A government program to digitally empower citizens

fields.explanation: Digital India is a government initiative to transform India into a digitally empowered society.

fields.text: Which of the following is a secondary storage device?
fields.explanation: Hard Disk is a secondary storage device that stores data permanently.

fields.text: What is the full form of ROM?
fields.explanation: ROM stands for Read Only Memory, which stores data permanently and cannot be modified.

fields.text: Which storage device has the fastest access time?
fields.explanation: SSD (Solid State Drive) has the fastest access time among the given storage devices.

fields.text: What is cache memory?

options.B: High-speed memory between CPU and RAM

fields.explanation: Cache memory is high-speed memory that stores frequently used data between CPU and RAM.

fields.text: How many bytes are in 1 Kilobyte?
fields.explanation: 1 Kilobyte (KB) equals 1024 bytes in computer memory systems.

fields.text: What is a computer virus?

options.B: A malicious program that replicates itself

fields.explanation: A computer virus is a malicious program that replicates itself and spreads to other computers.

fields.text: Which of the following is antivirus software?
fields.explanation: McAfee, Norton, and Kaspersky are all popular antivirus software programs.

fields.text: What is a Trojan horse in computing?
fields.explanation: A Trojan horse is malware that disguises itself as legitimate software to gain access to systems.

fields.text: What is two-factor authentication?
fields.explanation: Two-factor authentication requires two different verification methods for enhanced security.

fields.text: What is encryption?
fields.explanation: Encryption is the process of converting data into secret code to protect it from unauthorized access.

fields.text: Which programming language is primarily used for web development?
fields.explanation: JavaScript is primarily used for web development, especially for client-side scripting.

fields.text: What does OOP stand for in programming?
fields.explanation: OOP stands for Object Oriented Programming, a programming paradigm based on objects.

fields.text: Which programming language is used for artificial intelligence and machine learning?
fields.explanation: Python is widely used for artificial intelligence and machine learning applications.

fields.text: What is the extension of a Java source file?
fields.explanation: Java source files have the extension .java before compilation.

fields.text: What is a primary key in a database?

options.B: A unique identifier for each record

fields.explanation: A primary key is a unique identifier that distinguishes each record in a database table.

fields.text: Which SQL command is used to retrieve data from a database?
fields.explanation: SELECT is the SQL command used to retrieve data from a database table.

fields.text: What is a foreign key in a database?

options.B: A field that links to the primary key of another table

fields.explanation: A foreign key is a field that creates a link between two tables by referencing the primary key of another table.

fields.text: What does ACID stand for in database systems?
fields.explanation: ACID stands for Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability - properties of reliable database transactions.

fields.text: Which company provides Amazon Web Services (AWS)?
fields.explanation: Amazon Web Services (AWS) is a cloud computing platform provided by Amazon.

fields.text: What is virtualization in computing?

options.B: Creating virtual versions of physical resources

fields.explanation: Virtualization is the creation of virtual versions of physical computing resources like servers or storage.

fields.text: Which of the following is a cloud service model?
fields.explanation: SaaS (Software as a Service), PaaS (Platform as a Service), and IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) are all cloud service models.

fields.text: What is the main advantage of cloud computing?
fields.explanation: Cloud computing offers scalability and cost-effectiveness as its main advantages.

fields.text: What is hybrid cloud?

options.A: A mix of public and private clouds

fields.explanation: Hybrid cloud is a computing environment that combines public and private cloud infrastructures.

fields.text: Who is considered the father of Artificial Intelligence?
fields.explanation: John McCarthy is considered the father of Artificial Intelligence and coined the term "AI".

fields.text: What is deep learning?

options.B: A subset of machine learning using neural networks

fields.explanation: Deep learning is a subset of machine learning that uses artificial neural networks with multiple layers.

fields.text: What is natural language processing (NLP)?

options.A: A way to process natural languages
options.B: AI that helps computers understand human language

fields.explanation: NLP is a branch of AI that helps computers understand, interpret, and generate human language.

fields.text: What is a chatbot?

options.B: A computer program that simulates conversation

fields.explanation: A chatbot is a computer program designed to simulate conversation with human users.

fields.text: What is computer vision?

options.B: AI that enables computers to interpret visual information

fields.explanation: Computer vision is a field of AI that enables computers to interpret and understand visual information from images or videos.

fields.text: What does IoT enable?

options.A: Connecting physical devices to the internet

fields.explanation: IoT enables physical devices to connect to the internet and communicate with each other.

fields.text: Which of the following is an example of IoT device?
fields.explanation: Smart thermostat, connected car, and smart watch are all examples of IoT devices.

fields.text: What is a smart home?

options.B: A home with IoT devices for automation

fields.explanation: A smart home uses IoT devices to automate and control various home functions remotely.

fields.text: What is a sensor in IoT?

options.A: A device that detects changes in environment

fields.explanation: A sensor is a device that detects and responds to changes in its environment.

fields.text: What is edge computing in IoT?

options.A: Computing at the edge of a table
options.B: Processing data closer to where it's generated

fields.explanation: Edge computing processes data closer to where it's generated rather than in a centralized cloud.

fields.text: What is the first cryptocurrency?
fields.explanation: Bitcoin, created by Satoshi Nakamoto, was the first cryptocurrency.

fields.text: What is a blockchain?
fields.explanation: A blockchain is a distributed digital ledger that records transactions across multiple computers.

fields.text: What is cryptocurrency mining?

options.B: Process of validating transactions and creating new coins

fields.explanation: Cryptocurrency mining is the process of validating transactions and creating new cryptocurrency coins.

fields.text: What is a smart contract?

options.B: Self-executing contract with terms directly written into code

fields.explanation: A smart contract is a self-executing contract with terms of agreement directly written into code.

fields.text: What is decentralization in blockchain?
fields.explanation: Decentralization in blockchain means distributing control and data across multiple nodes rather than a single authority.

fields.text: What is a robot?

options.B: A programmable machine capable of carrying out tasks

fields.explanation: A robot is a programmable machine capable of carrying out a complex series of actions automatically.

fields.text: What is automation?

options.B: Use of technology to perform tasks without human intervention

fields.explanation: Automation is the use of technology to perform tasks with minimal human intervention.

fields.text: What is RPA?
fields.explanation: RPA stands for Robotic Process Automation, which uses software robots to automate repetitive tasks.

fields.text: What is an autonomous vehicle?

options.A: A vehicle that runs on its own

fields.explanation: An autonomous vehicle is a self-driving vehicle that can operate without human intervention using AI.

fields.text: What is industrial automation?

options.B: Use of robots and systems in manufacturing
options.C: Reducing human intervention in industrial processes

fields.explanation: Industrial automation involves using robots and control systems to reduce human intervention in industrial processes.

fields.text: What is Virtual Reality (VR)?
fields.explanation: VR is a computer-generated simulation of a three-dimensional environment that can be interacted with.

fields.text: What is Augmented Reality (AR)?

options.B: Overlaying digital information on real world

fields.explanation: AR overlays digital information and objects onto the real world through devices like smartphones or glasses.

fields.text: Which device is commonly used for VR?
fields.explanation: VR headset is the primary device used to experience virtual reality environments.

fields.text: What is Mixed Reality (MR)?

options.A: A mix of VR and AR
options.B: Blending of real and virtual worlds

fields.explanation: Mixed Reality (MR) combines elements of both VR and AR, blending real and virtual worlds for interactive experiences.

fields.text: Which field commonly uses VR for training?
fields.explanation: VR is commonly used in medical training, flight simulation, and military training for safe practice environments.

fields.text: What is NEFT?
fields.explanation: NEFT stands for National Electronic Funds Transfer, an electronic payment system in India.

fields.text: What is RTGS?
fields.explanation: RTGS stands for Real Time Gross Settlement, used for high-value electronic fund transfers.

fields.text: What is the maximum amount for UPI transactions per day?
fields.explanation: The maximum amount for UPI transactions per day is ₹1,00,000 for most banks.

fields.text: What is digital wallet?

options.B: Electronic device to store payment information

fields.explanation: A digital wallet is an electronic device or service that stores payment information and passwords.

fields.text: What is contactless payment?

options.B: Payment using NFC or RFID technology

fields.explanation: Contactless payment uses NFC or RFID technology for tap-and-pay transactions without physical contact.

fields.text: What is quantum computing?
fields.explanation: Quantum computing uses quantum mechanical phenomena like superposition and entanglement to process information.

fields.text: What is a qubit?

options.C: Can exist in multiple states simultaneously

fields.explanation: A qubit is the basic unit of quantum information that can exist in multiple states simultaneously.

fields.text: What is quantum supremacy?
fields.explanation: Quantum supremacy is the milestone where quantum computers outperform classical computers in specific tasks.

fields.text: Which company achieved quantum supremacy first?
fields.explanation: Google claimed to achieve quantum supremacy in 2019 with their Sycamore processor.

fields.text: What is quantum entanglement?
fields.explanation: Quantum entanglement is a phenomenon where quantum particles become connected and affect each other instantaneously.

fields.text: How many Fundamental Rights are guaranteed by the Indian Constitution?
fields.explanation: The Indian Constitution guarantees 6 Fundamental Rights after the 44th Amendment removed the Right to Property from the list of Fundamental Rights.

fields.text: Which Article of the Constitution deals with Right to Equality?
fields.explanation: Articles 14-18 collectively deal with Right to Equality. Article 14 provides equality before law, Article 15 prohibits discrimination, Article 16 ensures equality in public employment.

fields.text: The Right to Education is guaranteed under which Article?
fields.explanation: Article 21A, inserted by the 86th Amendment in 2002, guarantees free and compulsory education to all children aged 6-14 years.

fields.text: Which of the following is NOT a Fundamental Right?
fields.explanation: Right to Property was removed from Fundamental Rights by the 44th Amendment in 1978 and is now a legal right under Article 300A.

fields.text: The Right to Freedom of Religion is guaranteed under which Articles?
fields.explanation: Articles 25-28 deal with Right to Freedom of Religion, including freedom of conscience, practice, and propagation of religion.

fields.text: Which Article is known as the 'Heart and Soul' of the Constitution?
fields.explanation: Article 32 (Right to Constitutional Remedies) is called the 'Heart and Soul' of the Constitution by Dr. B.R. Ambedkar as it provides means to enforce other rights.

fields.text: The concept of Fundamental Duties was borrowed from which country's Constitution?
fields.explanation: The concept of Fundamental Duties was borrowed from the USSR Constitution and added to the Indian Constitution by the 42nd Amendment in 1976.

fields.text: How many Fundamental Duties are listed in the Constitution?
fields.explanation: There are 11 Fundamental Duties listed in Article 51A. Originally 10 duties were added by 42nd Amendment, and one more was added by 86th Amendment.

fields.text: Which Article deals with Fundamental Duties?
fields.explanation: Article 51A contains all 11 Fundamental Duties of Indian citizens.

fields.text: The Right to Life and Personal Liberty is guaranteed under which Article?
fields.explanation: Article 21 guarantees the Right to Life and Personal Liberty, which has been expanded by judicial interpretation to include many other rights.

fields.text: Which Amendment added Fundamental Duties to the Constitution?
fields.explanation: The 42nd Amendment Act of 1976 added 10 Fundamental Duties to the Constitution. The 86th Amendment later added one more duty.

fields.text: The Right to Privacy is recognized under which Article?
fields.explanation: The Supreme Court in K.S. Puttaswamy case (2017) recognized Right to Privacy as a fundamental right under Article 21.

fields.text: Which of the following rights is available to both citizens and non-citizens?
fields.explanation: Right to Equality (Articles 14-18) is available to all persons, while rights under Article 19 are available only to citizens.

fields.text: The Right against Exploitation is guaranteed under which Articles?
fields.explanation: Articles 23-24 guarantee Right against Exploitation, prohibiting human trafficking, forced labor, and employment of children in hazardous work.

fields.text: Which Article prohibits discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth?
fields.explanation: Article 15 specifically prohibits discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex, or place of birth.

fields.text: The practice of untouchability is prohibited under which Article?
fields.explanation: Article 17 abolishes untouchability and forbids its practice in any form.

fields.text: Which Article deals with abolition of titles?
fields.explanation: Article 18 abolishes titles except military and academic distinctions.

fields.text: The Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression is guaranteed under which clause of Article 19?
fields.explanation: Article 19(1)(a) guarantees the Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression to all citizens.

fields.text: Which of the following is NOT a reasonable restriction on freedom of speech?
fields.explanation: Personal opinion is not a reasonable restriction. The eight reasonable restrictions are: security of state, friendly relations with foreign states, public order, decency, morality, contempt of court, defamation, and incitement to offense.

fields.text: The Right to Form Associations is guaranteed under which Article?
fields.explanation: Article 19(1)(c) guarantees the Right to Form Associations or Unions.

fields.text: Which Article provides protection in respect of conviction for offenses?
fields.explanation: Article 20 provides protection against ex-post facto laws, double jeopardy, and self-incrimination.

fields.text: The concept of 'Double Jeopardy' is mentioned in which Article?
fields.explanation: Article 20(2) states that no person shall be prosecuted and punished for the same offense more than once.

fields.text: Which Article provides protection against arrest and detention?
fields.explanation: Article 22 provides protection against arrest and detention in certain cases.

fields.text: The Right to Constitutional Remedies empowers which court primarily?
fields.explanation: Article 32 empowers the Supreme Court to issue writs for enforcement of Fundamental Rights, though High Courts also have this power under Article 226.

fields.text: Which writ is issued when a person is illegally detained?
fields.explanation: Habeas Corpus is issued to secure the release of a person who has been illegally detained.

fields.text: The writ of 'Mandamus' is issued to:
fields.explanation: Mandamus is issued to compel a public authority to perform its legal duty.

fields.text: Which Article deals with Cultural and Educational Rights?
fields.explanation: Articles 29-30 deal with Cultural and Educational Rights of minorities.

fields.text: The duty to safeguard public property is mentioned in which part of Fundamental Duties?
fields.explanation: Article 51A(i) makes it a duty of every citizen to safeguard public property and to abjure violence.

fields.text: Which Fundamental Duty was added by the 86th Amendment?
fields.explanation: The 86th Amendment added the duty of parents/guardians to provide education to children between ages 6-14.

fields.text: The Right to Information Act is based on which Fundamental Right?
fields.explanation: The Right to Information is derived from Article 19(1)(a) - Freedom of Speech and Expression.

fields.text: Fundamental Rights can be suspended during:
fields.explanation: Fundamental Rights can be suspended only during National Emergency (Article 352), except Articles 20 and 21.

fields.text: Which Article empowers Parliament to modify Fundamental Rights?
fields.explanation: Article 368 gives Parliament the power to amend the Constitution, including Fundamental Rights, but subject to the basic structure doctrine.

fields.text: The doctrine of 'Basic Structure' was established in which case?
fields.explanation: The Basic Structure doctrine was established in Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973).

fields.text: Which of the following is a negative right?
fields.explanation: Right to Equality is a negative right as it requires the state to refrain from discrimination.

fields.text: The Right to Freedom of Religion does NOT include:
fields.explanation: The Right to Freedom of Religion does not include the right to convert others forcibly; it only includes freedom of conscience, practice, and propagation.

fields.text: Which Article prohibits forced labor?
fields.explanation: Article 23 prohibits traffic in human beings and forced labor.

fields.text: The minimum age for hazardous employment is:
fields.explanation: Article 24 prohibits employment of children below 14 years in factories, mines, or other hazardous employment.

fields.text: The Right to Establish Educational Institutions is guaranteed under:
fields.explanation: Article 30 gives minorities the right to establish and administer educational institutions.

fields.text: Fundamental Rights are:
fields.explanation: Fundamental Rights are both relative and qualified as they are subject to reasonable restrictions and can be suspended during emergencies.

fields.text: The concept of 'Procedure Established by Law' is mentioned in:
fields.explanation: Article 21 uses the phrase 'procedure established by law' which has been interpreted to mean 'due process of law' by the Supreme Court.

fields.text: Which committee recommended the inclusion of Fundamental Duties?
fields.explanation: The Swaran Singh Committee recommended the inclusion of Fundamental Duties in the Constitution.

fields.text: The Right to Move Freely is guaranteed under which Article?
fields.explanation: Article 19(1)(d) guarantees the right to move freely throughout the territory of India.

fields.text: The Right to Reside and Settle is guaranteed under which Article?
fields.explanation: Article 19(1)(e) guarantees the right to reside and settle in any part of the territory of India.

fields.text: The Right to Practice Any Profession is guaranteed under which Article?
fields.explanation: Article 19(1)(g) guarantees the right to practice any profession, or to carry on any occupation, trade or business.

fields.text: Which Article deals with the Right to Assemble Peacefully?
fields.explanation: Article 19(1)(b) guarantees the right to assemble peacefully and without arms.

fields.text: The writ of 'Prohibition' is issued to:
fields.explanation: Prohibition is issued by a higher court to prevent a lower court from exceeding its jurisdiction.

fields.text: The writ of 'Certiorari' is issued to:
fields.explanation: Certiorari is issued to quash the proceedings of a lower court when it has exceeded its jurisdiction.

fields.text: The writ of 'Quo-Warranto' is issued to:

options.B: Question a person's right to office

fields.explanation: Quo-Warranto is issued to question a person's right to hold a public office.

fields.text: Which Article provides that no child below 14 years shall be employed in hazardous work?
fields.explanation: Article 24 specifically prohibits employment of children below 14 years in factories, mines, or other hazardous employment.

fields.text: The Right to Freedom of Conscience is guaranteed under which Article?
fields.explanation: Article 25 guarantees freedom of conscience and the right to freely profess, practice, and propagate religion.

fields.text: Which Article provides freedom to manage religious affairs?
fields.explanation: Article 26 provides freedom to manage religious affairs, including the right to establish religious institutions.

fields.text: The provision 'No person shall be compelled to pay taxes for promotion of any religion' is in:
fields.explanation: Article 27 states that no person shall be compelled to pay any taxes for the promotion or maintenance of any particular religion.

fields.text: Which Article deals with freedom from religious instruction in educational institutions?
fields.explanation: Article 28 provides freedom from attending religious instruction or worship in educational institutions.

fields.text: The Right to Conserve Culture is guaranteed under which Article?
fields.explanation: Article 29 protects the interests of minorities and their right to conserve their distinct language, script, or culture.

fields.text: Which case established that the right to livelihood is part of Article 21?
fields.explanation: In Olga Tellis v. Bombay Municipal Corporation (1985), the Supreme Court held that right to livelihood is part of Article 21.

fields.text: The case that established 'due process of law' concept in India is:
fields.explanation: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978) established that 'procedure established by law' must be fair, just, and reasonable.

fields.text: Which Article empowers the state to make special provisions for women and children?
fields.explanation: Article 15(3) empowers the state to make special provisions for women and children.

fields.text: The 93rd Amendment is related to which Article?
fields.explanation: The 93rd Amendment added Article 15(5) to enable reservation in private educational institutions.

fields.text: Which Article provides for equality of opportunity in public employment?
fields.explanation: Article 16 provides for equality of opportunity in matters of public employment.

fields.text: The provision for reservation in promotion is covered under which Article?
fields.explanation: Article 16(4A), added by the 77th Amendment, provides for reservation in promotion for SCs and STs.

fields.text: The concept of 'Creamy Layer' is related to which Article?
fields.explanation: The 'Creamy Layer' concept was introduced in the context of Article 16 to exclude affluent sections from reservation benefits.

fields.text: Which Amendment Act removed the Right to Property from Fundamental Rights?
fields.explanation: The 44th Amendment Act of 1978 removed the Right to Property from the list of Fundamental Rights.

fields.text: The Right to Property is now a legal right under which Article?
fields.explanation: After the 44th Amendment, the Right to Property became a legal right under Article 300A.

fields.text: The doctrine of 'Eclipse' is related to which Article?
fields.explanation: The doctrine of Eclipse is related to Article 13, which deals with laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights.

fields.text: Which Article defines 'State' for the purpose of Fundamental Rights?
fields.explanation: Article 12 defines 'State' to include Government and Parliament of India, State Governments, local authorities, and other authorities.

fields.text: The power of judicial review is derived from which Article?
fields.explanation: Judicial review is derived from Articles 13, 32, and 226, which empower courts to review laws and executive actions.

fields.text: Which Article provides that laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights shall be void?
fields.explanation: Article 13 declares that laws inconsistent with Fundamental Rights shall be void to the extent of inconsistency.

fields.text: The term 'reasonable restrictions' first appeared in which case?
fields.explanation: The concept of 'reasonable restrictions' was first discussed in Romesh Thappar v. State of Madras (1950).

fields.text: How many types of writs can be issued under Article 32?
fields.explanation: Five types of writs can be issued: Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Certiorari, and Quo-Warranto.

fields.text: The Right to Speedy Trial is derived from which Article?
fields.explanation: The Right to Speedy Trial is derived from Article 21 as established in Hussainara Khatoon v. Home Secretary, Bihar (1979).

fields.text: The duty to develop scientific temper is mentioned in which Fundamental Duty?
fields.explanation: Article 51A(h) makes it a duty to develop scientific temper, humanism, and spirit of inquiry and reform.

fields.text: The duty to protect and improve the environment is mentioned in which Article?
fields.explanation: Article 51A(g) makes it a duty to protect and improve the natural environment including forests, lakes, rivers, and wildlife.

fields.text: The duty to strive towards excellence is mentioned in which Article?
fields.explanation: Article 51A(j) makes it a duty to strive towards excellence in all spheres of individual and collective activity.

fields.text: Which case established that Fundamental Rights are not absolute?
fields.explanation: Multiple cases have established that Fundamental Rights are not absolute and can be subject to reasonable restrictions.

fields.text: The Right to Health is derived from which Article?
fields.explanation: The Right to Health is derived from Article 21 as established in various Supreme Court judgments.

fields.text: The Right to Clean Environment is derived from which Article?
fields.explanation: The Right to Clean Environment is derived from Article 21 as established in M.C. Mehta cases.

fields.text: Which Article provides protection against self-incrimination?
fields.explanation: Article 20(3) provides protection against self-incrimination - no person can be compelled to be a witness against himself.

fields.text: The concept of 'Locus Standi' is related to which Article?
fields.explanation: Locus Standi (legal standing) is related to both Article 32 and Article 226 for filing writ petitions.

fields.text: Public Interest Litigation (PIL) is primarily based on which Article?
fields.explanation: PIL is based on Articles 32, 226 (for remedies) and often invokes Article 21 for substantive rights.

fields.text: The Right to Shelter is derived from which Article?
fields.explanation: The Right to Shelter is derived from Article 21 as established in Shantistar Builders v. Narayan Totame (1990).

fields.text: Which Article deals with ex-post facto laws?
fields.explanation: Article 20(1) prohibits ex-post facto laws - no person can be convicted for an act which was not an offense when committed.

fields.text: The Right to Legal Aid is derived from which Article?
fields.explanation: The Right to Legal Aid is derived from Article 21 as established in M.H. Hoskot v. State of Maharashtra (1978).

fields.text: Which Amendment is known as the 'Mini Constitution'?
fields.explanation: The 42nd Amendment (1976) is known as the 'Mini Constitution' as it made extensive changes to the Constitution.

fields.text: The Right to Compensation for acquisition of property is guaranteed under which Article?
fields.explanation: Article 300A guarantees that no person shall be deprived of property except by authority of law.

fields.text: Which Article provides for special provision for admission to educational institutions?
fields.explanation: Article 15(5), added by the 93rd Amendment, provides for special provisions in educational institutions including private institutions.

fields.text: The Right to be Defended by a lawyer is guaranteed under which Article?
fields.explanation: Article 22 guarantees the right to be defended by a legal practitioner of one's choice.

fields.text: Which Article provides protection against arbitrary detention?
fields.explanation: Both Article 21 and Article 22 provide protection against arbitrary detention.

fields.text: The maximum period of detention without trial under ordinary circumstances is:
fields.explanation: Under Article 22, a person cannot be detained for more than 24 hours without being produced before a magistrate.

fields.text: The Right to Sleep is derived from which Article?
fields.explanation: The Right to Sleep is derived from Article 21 as established in Ramlila Maidan case (2012).

fields.text: Which case established that the right to reputation is part of Article 21?
fields.explanation: In Subhash Sharma v. Union of India (1991), the Supreme Court held that right to reputation is part of Article 21.

fields.text: The Right to Food is derived from which Article?
fields.explanation: The Right to Food is derived from Article 21 as established in PUCL v. Union of India (2001).

fields.text: Which Article empowers Parliament to determine the authority for issuing writs?
fields.explanation: Article 35 empowers Parliament to make laws to give effect to certain provisions of Fundamental Rights.

fields.text: The enforcement of Fundamental Rights can be suspended during:
fields.explanation: Fundamental Rights can be suspended only during National Emergency under Article 352, except Articles 20 and 21.

fields.text: Which Articles cannot be suspended even during Emergency?
fields.explanation: Articles 20 and 21 cannot be suspended even during National Emergency as per the 44th Amendment.

fields.text: The Right to Dignity is derived from which Article?
fields.explanation: The Right to Dignity is derived from Article 21 as established in various Supreme Court judgments.

fields.text: Which case established that right to strike is not a fundamental right?
fields.explanation: In T.K. Rangarajan v. State of Tamil Nadu (2003), the Supreme Court held that right to strike is not a fundamental right.

fields.text: The Right to Know is derived from which Article?
fields.explanation: The Right to Know is derived from both Article 19(1)(a) and Article 21 as established in various cases.

fields.text: Which Article provides for special provisions for SC/ST in educational institutions?
fields.explanation: Article 15(4) empowers the state to make special provisions for SC/ST and other backward classes in educational institutions.

fields.text: The Directive Principles of State Policy are contained in which part of the Indian Constitution?
fields.explanation: The Directive Principles of State Policy are contained in Part IV of the Indian Constitution (Articles 36-51).

fields.text: Which article of the Constitution states that the State shall strive to promote the welfare of the people?
fields.explanation: Article 38 directs the State to strive to promote the welfare of the people by securing and protecting a social order in which justice shall inform all institutions of national life.

fields.text: The concept of Directive Principles of State Policy has been borrowed from which country's constitution?
fields.explanation: The concept of Directive Principles of State Policy has been borrowed from the Irish Constitution.

fields.text: Which article provides for the establishment of Panchayats in villages?
fields.explanation: Article 40 directs the State to organize village panchayats and endow them with such powers and authority as may be necessary to enable them to function as units of self-government.

fields.text: Article 39 of the Constitution deals with:
fields.explanation: Article 39 lays down certain principles of policy to be followed by the State, including adequate means of livelihood, prevention of concentration of wealth, and equal pay for equal work.

fields.text: Which article directs the State to provide free and compulsory education for children?
fields.explanation: Article 45 (now modified by the 86th Amendment) originally directed the State to provide free and compulsory education for all children until they complete 14 years of age.

fields.text: Article 44 of the Constitution deals with:
fields.explanation: Article 44 directs the State to endeavour to secure for the citizens a uniform civil code throughout the territory of India.

fields.text: Which article prohibits the consumption of intoxicating drinks and drugs?
fields.explanation: Article 47 directs the State to bring about prohibition of the consumption of intoxicating drinks and drugs which are injurious to health.

fields.text: Article 48 of the Constitution deals with:

options.B: Organization of agriculture and animal husbandry

fields.explanation: Article 48 directs the State to organize agriculture and animal husbandry on modern and scientific lines and to improve the breeds of cattle and other useful animals.

fields.text: The prohibition of cow slaughter is mentioned in which article?
fields.explanation: Article 48 also prohibits the slaughter of cows and other useful cattle, along with organizing agriculture and animal husbandry.

fields.text: Article 48A was added to the Constitution by which amendment?
fields.explanation: Article 48A, which deals with protection and improvement of environment and forests, was added by the 42nd Constitutional Amendment in 1976.

fields.text: Which article deals with the protection of monuments and places of national importance?
fields.explanation: Article 49 obligates the State to protect monuments and places and objects of national importance against spoliation, disfigurement, destruction, removal, disposal or export.

fields.text: Article 50 of the Constitution deals with:
fields.explanation: Article 50 directs the State to separate the judiciary from the executive in the public services of the State.

fields.text: The promotion of international peace and security is mentioned in which article?
fields.explanation: Article 51 directs the State to promote international peace and security, maintain just and honorable relations between nations, and encourage settlement of international disputes by arbitration.

fields.text: Which of the following is NOT a Directive Principle of State Policy?
fields.explanation: Right to constitutional remedies is a Fundamental Right (Article 32), not a Directive Principle of State Policy.

fields.text: Article 39A was added to the Constitution by which amendment?
fields.explanation: Article 39A, which deals with equal justice and free legal aid, was added by the 42nd Constitutional Amendment in 1976.

fields.text: Which article deals with nutrition and standard of living?
fields.explanation: Article 47 directs the State to raise the level of nutrition and the standard of living and to improve public health.

fields.text: Article 43 of the Constitution deals with:
fields.explanation: Article 43 directs the State to secure a living wage, decent standard of life, and social and cultural opportunities for all workers, including provisions for maternity relief.

fields.text: The participation of workers in management of industries is mentioned in which article?
fields.explanation: Article 43A directs the State to take steps to secure participation of workers in the management of undertakings, establishments or other organizations.

fields.text: Which article deals with the welfare of Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes?
fields.explanation: Article 46 directs the State to promote the educational and economic interests of the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes and protect them from social injustice.

fields.text: The Directive Principles of State Policy are:
fields.explanation: The Directive Principles of State Policy are non-justiciable, meaning they cannot be enforced by courts, though they are fundamental in governance.

fields.text: Which article states that DPSPs are fundamental in governance?
fields.explanation: Article 37 states that the Directive Principles are fundamental in the governance of the country, though not enforceable by courts.

fields.text: Article 41 of the Constitution deals with:
fields.explanation: Article 41 directs the State to secure the right to work, education, and public assistance in cases of unemployment, old age, sickness, and disablement.

fields.text: Which article deals with just and humane conditions of work?
fields.explanation: Article 42 directs the State to make provision for securing just and humane conditions of work and for maternity relief.

fields.text: The term 'State' in Directive Principles includes:
fields.explanation: As per Article 36, the term 'State' in DPSPs has the same meaning as in Part III (Fundamental Rights) and includes both Central and State Governments.

fields.text: Which of the following is a Gandhian Principle among DPSPs?
fields.explanation: Articles 47 (prohibition), 43 (cottage industries), and 48 (cow slaughter prohibition) are considered Gandhian Principles among DPSPs.

fields.text: The idea of 'Welfare State' is reflected in which part of the Constitution?
fields.explanation: The Directive Principles of State Policy reflect the idea of a 'Welfare State' by directing the State to work for the welfare of the people.

fields.text: Article 51A deals with:
fields.explanation: Article 51A deals with Fundamental Duties, not Directive Principles. Article 51 deals with international peace and security.

fields.text: The Constitution originally contained how many Directive Principles?
fields.explanation: The Constitution originally contained 14 Directive Principles. Later, Articles 39A and 43A were added through the 42nd Amendment.

fields.text: Which case established the supremacy of Fundamental Rights over Directive Principles?
fields.explanation: The Champakam Dorairajan case (1951) established that Fundamental Rights have supremacy over Directive Principles in case of conflict.

fields.text: Article 43B was added to the Constitution by which amendment?
fields.explanation: Article 43B, which deals with promotion of cooperative societies, was added by the 97th Constitutional Amendment in 2011.

fields.text: Which article deals with equal justice and free legal aid?
fields.explanation: Article 39A directs the State to promote equal justice and provide free legal aid to ensure that opportunities for securing justice are not denied to any citizen.

fields.text: The minimum age for marriage is indirectly mentioned in which article?
fields.explanation: Article 39(e) and (f) indirectly refer to child marriage by stating that children should not be forced into occupations unsuited to their age and should have healthy development.

fields.text: Which principle is NOT directly mentioned in the Directive Principles?
fields.explanation: Right to privacy is not mentioned in the Directive Principles. It was later recognized as a Fundamental Right by the Supreme Court.

fields.text: Article 48A deals with:
fields.explanation: Article 48A directs the State to protect and improve the environment and to safeguard the forests and wildlife of the country.

fields.text: The promotion of Hindi language is mentioned in which article?
fields.explanation: Article 351 directs the Union to promote the spread of Hindi language and develop it as the medium of expression for all elements of the composite culture of India.

fields.text: Which of the following is a Socialist Principle among DPSPs?
fields.explanation: Article 39(a) regarding adequate means of livelihood for all citizens is classified as a Socialist Principle among DPSPs.

fields.text: The duty to raise the level of nutrition is mentioned in which article?
fields.explanation: Article 47 specifically directs the State to raise the level of nutrition and the standard of living and to improve public health.

fields.text: Which article deals with the protection of weaker sections of society?
fields.explanation: Article 46 specifically directs the State to promote the educational and economic interests of Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, and other weaker sections.

fields.text: The obligation to respect international law is mentioned in which article?
fields.explanation: Article 51 directs the State to respect international law and treaty obligations in its dealings with other nations.

fields.text: Which amendment made education a fundamental right?
fields.explanation: The 86th Constitutional Amendment (2002) made education a fundamental right by adding Article 21A and modified Article 45.

fields.text: Article 39(d) deals with:
fields.explanation: Article 39(d) directs the State to ensure equal pay for equal work for both men and women.

fields.text: The concept of 'economic democracy' is reflected in which article?
fields.explanation: Article 43A, which deals with worker participation in management, reflects the concept of economic democracy.

fields.text: Which article is known as the 'heart and soul' of the Constitution according to Dr. B.R. Ambedkar?
fields.explanation: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar called Article 32 (Right to Constitutional Remedies) the 'heart and soul' of the Constitution, though this is not a DPSP.

fields.text: The promotion of cottage industries is mentioned in which article?
fields.explanation: Article 43 directs the State to promote cottage industries on an individual or cooperative basis in rural areas.

fields.text: Which of the following is NOT included in Article 51?
fields.explanation: Protection of national monuments is covered under Article 49, not Article 51 which deals with international relations.

fields.text: The term 'living wage' is mentioned in which article?
fields.explanation: Article 43 directs the State to secure a living wage for all workers, ensuring a decent standard of life and full enjoyment of leisure.

fields.text: Which article deals with free legal aid?
fields.explanation: Article 39A specifically directs the State to provide free legal aid to ensure equal justice and access to courts for all citizens.

fields.text: The maximum hours of work for adults is indirectly mentioned in which article?
fields.explanation: Article 42 deals with just and humane conditions of work, which indirectly includes provisions for reasonable working hours.

fields.text: Which principle emphasizes decentralization of power?
fields.explanation: Article 40, which deals with organization of village panchayats, emphasizes decentralization of power to the grassroots level.

fields.text: Article 47 does NOT include:
fields.explanation: Free education for children is covered under Article 45, not Article 47 which deals with nutrition, health, and prohibition.

fields.text: The protection of tenure of judges is mentioned in which article?
fields.explanation: Article 50 directs separation of judiciary from executive, which indirectly includes protection of judicial independence and tenure.

fields.text: Which article deals with maternity relief?
fields.explanation: Article 42 specifically mentions provision for maternity relief along with just and humane conditions of work.

fields.text: The promotion of scientific temper is mentioned in which part of the Constitution?
fields.explanation: The promotion of scientific temper is mentioned in Article 51A (Fundamental Duties), not in Directive Principles.

fields.text: Which article deals with ancient monuments and archaeological sites?
fields.explanation: Article 49 obligates the State to protect monuments and places of national importance from damage and destruction.

fields.text: The prevention of concentration of wealth is mentioned in which sub-clause of Article 39?
fields.explanation: Article 39(c) directs that the operation of the economic system should not result in the concentration of wealth and means of production.

fields.text: Which case established the doctrine of harmonious construction between Fundamental Rights and DPSPs?
fields.explanation: Both Kesavananda Bharati (1973) and Minerva Mills (1980) cases established the principle of harmonious construction between Fundamental Rights and DPSPs.

fields.text: Article 39(e) deals with:
fields.explanation: Article 39(e) directs that the health and strength of workers, men and women, should not be abused and they should not be forced into occupations unsuited to their age or strength.

fields.text: The idea of 'Right to Work' is mentioned in which article?
fields.explanation: Article 41 directs the State to secure the right to work and make provision for securing the right to work in cases of unemployment.

fields.text: Which article emphasizes the welfare of the people through social order?
fields.explanation: Article 38 directs the State to strive to promote the welfare of the people by securing and protecting a social order in which justice shall inform all institutions of national life.

fields.text: hellow
fields.explanation: dfsd

fields.text: What is the capital of France?
fields.explanation: Paris is the capital of France.

fields.text: Which language is used for web development?
fields.explanation: HTML is a markup language used to build websites.

fields.text: What is the square root of 49?
fields.explanation: The square root of 49 is 7.

fields.text: In which year did India gain independence?
fields.explanation: India gained independence in 1947.

fields.text: Which gas do plants absorb?
fields.explanation: Plants absorb carbon dioxide for photosynthesis.

fields.text: Which continent is the Sahara Desert located in?
fields.explanation: The Sahara is in Africa.

fields.text: What is H2O commonly known as?
fields.explanation: H2O is the chemical formula for water.

fields.text: Who is known as the father of the modern Kerala renaissance?
fields.explanation: Sri Narayana Guru is widely regarded as the father of the Kerala renaissance for his efforts to reform the caste-ridden society of Kerala.

fields.text: The state of Kerala was formed on which date?
fields.explanation: The States Reorganisation Act of 1956 led to the formation of the state of Kerala on November 1, 1956.

fields.text: Which of the following is responsible for the red color of human blood?
fields.explanation: Hemoglobin is a protein in red blood cells that contains iron and binds with oxygen, giving blood its characteristic red color.

fields.text: What is the chemical formula for water?
fields.explanation: Water is a chemical compound consisting of two hydrogen atoms bonded to a single oxygen atom.

fields.text: If a shop offers a 20% discount on an item marked at Rs. 500, what is the selling price?
fields.explanation: The discount is 20% of 500, which is (20/100) * 500 = Rs. 100. The selling price is the marked price minus the discount, so 500 - 100 = Rs. 400.

fields.text: കേരളത്തിലെ ഏറ്റവും വലിയ ശുദ്ധജല തടാകം ഏത്?
fields.explanation: ശാസ്താംകോട്ട തടാകമാണ് കേരളത്തിലെ ഏറ്റവും വലിയ ശുദ്ധജല തടാകം. കൊല്ലം ജില്ലയിലാണ് ഇത് സ്ഥിതി ചെയ്യുന്നത്.

fields.text: ഇന്ത്യൻ നാഷണൽ കോൺഗ്രസിന്റെ ആദ്യ വനിതാ പ്രസിഡന്റ് ആര്?
fields.explanation: ഇന്ത്യൻ നാഷണൽ കോൺഗ്രസിന്റെ ആദ്യ വനിതാ പ്രസിഡന്റ് ആനി ബസന്റ് ആയിരുന്നു. അവർ 1917-ൽ കൊൽക്കത്ത സമ്മേളനത്തിൽ അധ്യക്ഷയായി.

fields.text: 1857-ലെ കലാപത്തെ ഇന്ത്യയുടെ ഒന്നാം സ്വാതന്ത്ര്യസമരം എന്ന് വിശേഷിപ്പിച്ചത് ആര്?
fields.explanation: 1857-ലെ ശിപായി ലഹളയെ 'ഇന്ത്യയുടെ ഒന്നാം സ്വാതന്ത്ര്യസമരം' എന്ന് വിശേഷിപ്പിച്ചത് വി.ഡി. സവർക്കർ ആണ്. അദ്ദേഹത്തിന്റെ പ്രശസ്തമായ പുസ്തകമാണ് 'The Indian War of Independence 1857'.

fields.text: ഇന്ത്യയിലെ ഏറ്റവും വലിയ കണ്ടൽവന പ്രദേശം ഏത്?
fields.explanation: ഇന്ത്യയിലെ ഏറ്റവും വലിയ കണ്ടൽവന പ്രദേശം സുന്ദർബൻസ് ആണ്. ഇത് പശ്ചിമ ബംഗാളിലാണ് സ്ഥിതി ചെയ്യുന്നത്.

fields.text: കേരളത്തിലെ സമുദ്രനിരപ്പിൽ താഴെ സ്ഥിതിചെയ്യുന്ന പ്രദേശം ഏത്?
fields.explanation: കേരളത്തിലെ സമുദ്രനിരപ്പിൽ താഴെ സ്ഥിതിചെയ്യുന്ന പ്രദേശമാണ് കുട്ടനാട്. ആലപ്പുഴ, കോട്ടയം ജില്ലകളിലായി ഇത് വ്യാപിച്ചുകിടക്കുന്നു.

fields.text: അശോകചക്രത്തിൽ എത്ര ആരക്കാലുകൾ ഉണ്ട്?
fields.explanation: ഇന്ത്യയുടെ ദേശീയ പതാകയിലെ അശോകചക്രത്തിൽ 24 ആരക്കാലുകൾ ഉണ്ട്. ഇത് ധർമ്മത്തെയും ചലനത്തെയും സൂചിപ്പിക്കുന്നു.

fields.text: മഹാത്മാഗാന്ധി ആദ്യമായി സത്യാഗ്രഹം ആരംഭിച്ചത് എവിടെയാണ്?
fields.explanation: മഹാത്മാഗാന്ധി ആദ്യമായി സത്യാഗ്രഹം ആരംഭിച്ചത് ദക്ഷിണാഫ്രിക്കയിലാണ്, വർണ്ണവിവേചനത്തിനെതിരെ ആയിരുന്നു അത്. ഇന്ത്യയിലെ ആദ്യത്തെ സത്യാഗ്രഹം ചമ്പാരനിലാണ്.

fields.text: കേരള നിയമസഭയിൽ എത്ര അംഗങ്ങൾ ഉണ്ട്?
fields.explanation: കേരള നിയമസഭയിൽ ആകെ 140 അംഗങ്ങൾ ഉണ്ട് (139 തിരഞ്ഞെടുക്കപ്പെട്ടവരും ഒരാൾ ആംഗ്ലോ-ഇന്ത്യൻ പ്രതിനിധിയും).

content_data.question_text: The term 'Grand Slam' is associated with:**
content_data.explanation: Grand Slam in tennis refers to winning all four major tournaments in a single year.*

content_data.question_text: Which of the following is a fungal disease in livestock?
content_data.explanation: Ringworm, caused by fungi like Trichophyton, affects livestock skin. The others are caused by bacteria or protozoa.

content_data.question_text: Number of locations that could be addressed by a microprocessor with 12 address lines:
content_data.explanation: A microprocessor with 12 address lines can address 2^12 = 4096 memory locations.

content_data.question_text: The first law of thermodynamics for a closed system is:
content_data.explanation: The first law for a closed system states that change in internal energy equals heat added minus work done by the system.
Aryabhata was India's first satellite, launched on April 19, 1975, by the Soviet Union.

The Keoladeo National Park is located in:
content_data.explanation: Keoladeo National Park (formerly Bharatpur Bird Sanctuary) is located in Rajasthan and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

content_data.question_text: The highest court in India is:
content_data.explanation: The Supreme Court of India is the highest judicial authority in the country, established under the Indian Constitution.
ROM (Read-Only Memory) is non-volatile, retaining data without power.

content_data.question_text: What is the most common cause of acute pancreatitis?
content_data.explanation: Gallstones are the most common cause of acute pancreatitis, followed by alcohol abuse, accounting for about 80% of cases.

content_data.fact_text: Palakkad is the largest district in Kerala by area.
Alappuzha is the smallest district in Kerala by area.
Malappuram is the most populous district in Kerala.
Wayanad is the least populous district in Kerala.
Periyar is the longest river in Kerala with a length of 244 km.
